title: Understanding Why Your Dog Is Struggling to Rise from Their Back Legs
meta_desc: Is your dog having trouble standing up? Learn about hind limb weakness causes, when to call the vet, and how to help your dog today.
content_html:
What Is Hind Limb Weakness?

Difficulty rising from the back legs, often referred to as hind limb weakness or paresis, occurs when a dog struggles to support their weight or coordinate movement in their rear end. You may notice your dog 'bunny-hopping,' trembling while standing, or using their front legs to pull themselves up. This condition is often a sign of underlying discomfort, joint inflammation, or nerve interference.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for difficulty rising is typically Medium. If your dog is still able to walk once they are up and is eating and drinking normally, you should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. However, if the weakness is sudden, if they are dragging their legs, or if they seem to be in significant pain (panting, whining, or unable to settle), this can escalate to an emergency situation requiring immediate care.

How Photos and Videos Help Triage

Because dogs often hide their symptoms or act differently due to adrenaline at the vet clinic, capturing a video of the behavior at home is invaluable. A video of your dog attempting to rise or walking on a flat surface helps the veterinary team differentiate between an orthopedic issue (like arthritis or a ligament tear) and a neurological issue (like a slipped disc). Clear photos of your dog’s standing posture can also help identify muscle wasting or abnormal joint angles.

faqs.question: Is this just a normal part of my dog getting older?
faqs.answer: While it is common in senior dogs, it is not considered 'normal.' It is usually a sign of manageable pain from conditions like arthritis. Treatment can significantly improve their mobility and quality of life.

faqs.question: What are common causes of back leg weakness?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include osteoarthritis, hip dysplasia, Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), and cranial cruciate ligament tears.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog over-the-counter human pain relief?
faqs.answer: No. Many human medications, such as Ibuprofen or Naproxen, are highly toxic to dogs. Only use medications specifically prescribed by your veterinarian.
